coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate by carbon quantum dots: rational regulation of surface functionality and computational insights
coredata.dc:creator: Han, W.
Li, D.

Sun, H.
Wang, S.
Duan, X.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging carbonaceous photocatalysts, but the efficiency is unsatisfactory due to a narrow visible-light response and high electron-hole recombination. In this work, we fine-tuned the surface chemistry of CQDs via selectively substituting surface carbonyl group (C=O) with phenylhydrazine (PH). Such a modification optimized the conductivity and band structures of the derived products (CQDs-PH) with improved light absorption and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, leading to boosted generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) via peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. In addition, the primary ROS are identified as superoxide radicals (O2 •−) and holes, whereas O2 •− are produced from CQDs-activated PMS rather than from dissolved oxygen. Theoretical calculations illustrate that the electrons and holes on CQDs are spatially separated due to the spontaneous surface polarization induced by PH functionalization. This study provides a feasible approach to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CQDs for sustainable water decontamination.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activation of peroxymonosulfate by carbon quantum dots: rational regulation of surface functionality and computational insights

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging carbonaceous photocatalysts, but the efficiency is unsatisfactory due to a narrow visible-light response and high electron-hole recombination. In this work, we fine-tuned the surface chemistry of CQDs via selectively substituting surface carbonyl group (C=O) with phenylhydrazine (PH). Such a modification optimized the conductivity and band structures of the derived products (CQDs-PH) with improved light absorption and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, leading to boosted generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) via peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. In addition, the primary ROS are identified as superoxide radicals (O2 •−) and holes, whereas O2 •− are produced from CQDs-activated PMS rather than from dissolved oxygen. Theoretical calculations illustrate that the electrons and holes on CQDs are spatially separated due to the spontaneous surface polarization induced by PH functionalization. This study provides a feasible approach to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CQDs for sustainable water decontamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes are extensively used in plastics, textile and papermaking industries to bring vivid colors to daily commodities. With the vigorous development of the dyeing industry, the discharged volume of organic dye-containing wastewater is increasing progressively. However, dye-containing wastewater has obvious colors even at a low concentration, which reduces the penetration of sunlight in water, hinders the growth of aquatic living species, and causes potential damage to the natural environment [1–4]. However, most commercial dyes are resistant to conventional wastewater treatment technologies such as physical separation and biodegradation.

In recent years, persulfate-based advanced oxidation processes (PS-AOPs) have attracted tremendous interest in water purification, leveraging the produced highly reactive oxygen species (ROS). Compared with the traditional Fenton reaction, sulfate radical (SO4 •−) in PS-AOPs is featured by its high oxidation potential (2.5–3.2 V), pH independence, selectivity, and long lifespan (30–40 μs) in water treatment [5–7]. Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) is an effective oxidant to generate SO4 •− because of the asymmetry and small-molecule structure. Many methods have been used to activate PMS, including thermal activation [8], UV irradiation [9], electrochemical activation [10,11], alkali activation [12], transition metals [13] and nonmetals [14,15]. PMS-based systems can effectively degrade various organic compounds, such as organic dyes [16], phenols [17], bisphenol A [18], antibiotics [19] and pesticides [20].

Distinct from metal-based catalysis and energy-intensive physical stimuli, carbon materials are appealing in persulfate chemistry due to their unique nanostructure, chemical stability, strong adsorption capacity, and low toxicity [21,22]. Currently, graphene [23,24], carbon fiber [25,26], porous carbon [27,28], carbon nanotubes [29,30], nanodiamonds [31,32] and carbon nanobubbles [33,34] have been studied as green catalysts to activate PMS. Despite the high efficiency of metal-free systems, the poor stability of carbonaceous materials in the oxidative environment and the low mineralisation efficiency caused by the mild nonradical pathway of carbocatalysis have limited their practical application [35,36]. As a metal-free and visible light-sensitive nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emerge as a green and alternative photocatalyst to traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots. CQDs are featured by strong photoluminescence, stability, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, excellent water dispersibility, and rich and adjustable surface functionalities, rendering them promising candidates in photocatalysis [37–39]. The bandgap of CQDs can be adjusted by the energy difference between the surface state and the valence band. However, the disadvantages of pristine CQDs are the unsatisfactory absorption of visible light, rapid electron-hole recombination, and poor conductivity for charge separation. To improve the photocatalytic performance of CQDs, non-metal elements such as B, Cl, S and P have been doped into the carbon matrix to optimize the properties of CQDs by creating rich surface defects, which help extend light absorption, inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs, and improve photocatalytic performance [40–43]. However, the application of precisely modified CQDs in PS-AOPs is less explored.

In this study, CQDs with enriched oxygen functional groups were prepared via facile and one-step pyrolysis of maltitol and hydrogen peroxide, and the surface chemistry of CQDs was further tailored by phenylhydrazine (PH). The photocatalytic activity of PH-modified CQDs (CQDs-PH) was significantly improved for PMS activation and degradation of organic dyes. The primary ROS were determined by competitive radical quenching experiments. The physical, chemical, optical and electrochemical properties of CQDs were clarified by diverse characterisation/analysis techniques. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations at both ground and excited states were performed to predict the hole and electron distributions of CQDs and CQDs-PH.

Maltitol, PMS, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and methanol (MA, 99%) were of analytical grade and purchased from McLean Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) aqueous solution, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), trichloromethane (CHCl3), methylene blue (MB) and ammonium oxalate (AMO, 99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl), phenylhydrazine (PH, 99%), tert-butanol (TBA, 99%), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 99%), L-histidine (His, 98%), and silver nitrate (SN, 99%) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were used without further purification. Deionised water with the resistivity of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used for all experiments.

CQDs. First, a mixture of 5 g maltitol and 7 mL hydrogen peroxide solution was placed in a 100 mL beaker and sonicated for 3 min to aid the dissolution process. The solution was then transferred to a glass petri dish and placed in a blast drying oven at 200 °C for 15 min to obtain roughly 4.28 g solid carbon dots (CQDs) in reddish-brown. In this process, all H2O2 was consumed, and water was evaporated.

CQDs-PH. Generally, 2 g phenylhydrazine (PH) and 2 g CQDs were added to 100 mL CHCl3, and then 100 μL HCl (38%) was added to adjust the solution pH to 4. After stirring under N2 protection for 24 h, the precipitate was extracted with CHCl3 several times to remove the unreacted PH molecules. The obtained aqueous solution was dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h to yield CQDs-PH.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the carbon materials were obtained on a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer (US) using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on a Kratos Axis UltraDLD spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were tested on a Brucker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (Germany). Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM HR800 spectrometer (France). Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were obtained on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM (US) operating at 200 kV. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450PC spectrophotometer (Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were conducted on a Hitachi F-4600 luminescence spectrometer (Japan). Transient photocurrent and Mott-Schottky measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation CHI660D (China). The as-prepared CQDs and CQDs-PH were used as the working electrode; Pt wire was used as the counter electrode; a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode. The electrolyte was aqueous 0.05 M Na2SO4. The working electrode was irradiated under visible light from a 300 W Xe lamp (BoPhilae Technology Co., LTD, Beijing) with a 420 nm cutoff filter. Chopped-light amperometric I-t characterizations were recorded under chopped light irradiation. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) profiles were recorded under an AC voltage amplitude of 5 mV within a frequency range from 105 to 0.01 Hz with 50 mM Na2SO4.

Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations were performed with Gaussian 16 for different descriptors of various systems. The ground state geometry was optimized at B3LYP/6-311 g (d, p) with an SDD pseudo potential. Meanwhile, aqueous solutions are considered by the solvation SMD model. The TD-DFT was inspected with the CAM-B3LYP/6-311 g (d, p) level based on the optimized structure, and the Multiwfn was used to analyze the distribution of electrons and holes in different excited states [44,45]. The adsorption energy (Eads) was calculated to evaluate the ability of CQDs to interact and activate PMS, as follows (Eq. (1)):

where ECQDs/PMS, ECQDs and EPMS are the total energy of CQDs with the adsorbed PMS, CQDs without the adsorbed PMS, and free PMS, respectively.

The photocatalytic experiment was evaluated using a 40 W light-emitting diode (LED) light (Philips Investment Co., LTD, China) with wavelengths ranging from 450 to 460 nm. Before irradiation and addition of PMS, a certain amount of photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL MB aqueous solution with magnetically stirring for 30 min in the dark to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The distance between the light source and the liquid surface was about 10 cm. Then, PMS was quickly added to the solution to trigger the photocatalytic experiment after turning on the light. The initial pH of the reaction solution was adjusted using 0.1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M NaOH. At designated time intervals, 3 mL solution was taken and mixed with sodium sulfite (NaSO3) to quench the reaction. The concentration of MB was analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 664 nm. The concentration of residual PMS was measured by an iodometric titration method (Text S1). Triplicate experiments were carried out, and the standard deviation data were presented.

The synthesis procedure of CQDs and CQDs-PH is demonstrated in Fig. 1 a. Briefly, high-quality CQDs were successfully produced via thermal decomposition of maltitol and hydrogen peroxide. First, hydrogen peroxide acted as an oxidizing reagent to carbonize maltitol and induce diverse oxygen functional groups. Then, the as-prepared CQDs were further treated with PH for selective reaction with the ketonic group (-C=O) to form –C=N–NH-benzene functionality (CQDs-PH) under acidic conditions [46].

As revealed from the TEM images (Fig. 1b), monodisperse CQDs were successfully fabricated with a narrow size distribution between 3.0 and 5.0 nm and a mean particle diameter of 4.1 nm. The lattice of CQDs can be clearly resolved from the HRTEM image in Fig. 1c, showing good crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphite. Compared with CQDs, the TEM images (Fig. 1d and e) demonstrate that the surface modification with PH did not change the particle size, morphology and graphitic structures of CQDs.

Raman spectra of CQDs and CQDs-PH present two characteristic scattering modes: D-band (carbon defect) and G-band (sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon). The intensity ratio of the D-band to G-band is conventionally known as an index of the defective degree of carbonaceous materials. As shown in Fig. S1, the ID/IG value of CQDs (1.06) is identical to CQDs-PH (1.06), confirming that PH modification would not induce new structural or surface defects. In addition, N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S2) of CQDs and CQDs-PH were similar, and CQDs and CQDs-PH had identical BET specific surface areas. These results revealed that the introduction of PH on the CQDs surface did not induce noticeable changes in texture.

The FTIR spectra of CQDs are depicted in Fig. 2 a, and the stretching frequencies at 3,418, 2,933, 1,730 and 1,077 cm−1 are ascribed to –OH, -C-H, C=O and C–O–C, respectively. Compared with CQDs, CQDs-PH shows a significantly reduced vibration peak for C=O owing to the consumption of C=O, and a new vibration peak at 1,670 cm−1 referring to the formation of C=N bond on CQDs during the titration reaction [47,48]. Two strong vibrational peaks appear at 756 and 692 cm−1, which are attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of C–H on the benzene ring in the PH group. In addition, the vibration peak wavenumber of the C=C in the aromatic ring skeleton is shifted from 1,639 to 1,603 cm−1 with increased intensity. This may be due to the conjugation effect formed between the PH group and CQDs, where the electrons in were localized with enhanced polarity, resulting in a decrease in the stretching vibration frequency with an absorption intensity. Such a change would facilitate the rapid transport of photogenerated carriers to improve the photocatalytic activity. XPS measurements were performed to further verify the surface chemical compositions and states of the as-prepared CQDs and CQDs-PH. As displayed in the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2b), C, N and O elements can be observed for CQDs-PH at 284.3, 397.0 and 530.2 eV, respectively, while only C and O were observed for CQDs. Furthermore, the peak areas obtained through the deconvolution of O1s XPS spectra could reflect the surface concentrations of different O species (Fig. 2c) [49]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s shows four peaks at 532.7, 531.5, 530.7, and 530.1 eV, corresponding to H2Oabs, –OH/C–O–C, COOH and C=O, respectively. The declined intensity of the C=O signal for CQDs-PH indicated that C=O was consumed upon reaction with PH. The presence of N in CQDs-PH is further verified by the high-resolution N1s spectra in Fig. 2d, demonstrating that CQDs have been functionalized by PH. Compared with CQDs, the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d) of CQDs-PH showed a peak attributed to the C=N bond [50]. The detailed elemental compositions of the two samples are summarized in Table S1.

The optical properties of the as-synthesized CQDs and CQDs-PH were investigated by photoluminescence (PL) and UV–vis absorption spectra. The PL spectra of CQDs and CQDs-PH varied at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 460 nm (Fig. 3 a and b), exhibiting a typical excitation-dependent PL phenomenon [51]. Such an excitation-dependent PL behavior is determined by the plenteous emissive traps of surface chemical states triggered by different functional groups (C=O, C=N and C–OH) [52]. Different functional groups may generate distinct emissive traps under specific excitation wavelengths, giving rise to the excitation-dependent PL shift. Compared with CQDs only peaking at 445 nm, CQDs-PH shows the strongest emission peak at 459 nm and a second excitation at 360 nm. When the excitation wavelength changes from 280 to 460 nm, the emission peak of CQDs-PH has an obvious red shift and declined intensity compared with CQDs, indicating that the two samples possessed different surface states. In addition, compared with CQDs (2.42%), the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs-PH reduced to 0.47% using quinine sulfate (QY = 54%, 0.1 M H2SO4, 420 nm) as the reference (Table S2). These outcomes imply that PH successfully reacted with CQDs to generate different emission traps via forming a new surface functional group of C=N, thereby exhibiting different emission properties [53].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs and CQDs-PH were depicted in Fig. 3c. CQDs-PH presents a more intensive absorption range, which contributes to the light-harvesting capacity of CQDs-PH. A strong UV–Vis absorption peak appeared at 285 nm, corresponding to π-π∗ transition of C=C bond and n-π∗ transition of C=O bond. A weak absorption band from 350 to 400 nm was observed, attributed to n-π∗ transition of C=N bond on CQDs-PH. In addition, CQDs-PH exhibits weaker green luminescence than CQDs under 365 nm UV light illumination (Fig. 3c inset), indicating that the two samples possessed different surface states. The optical bandgaps of the two samples were estimated by the Kubelka-Munk theory (Text S3). The bandgap values of CQDs and CQDs-PH were calculated from the linear extrapolation using the Tauc plot (Fig. 3d). CQDs-PH (2.9–3.89 eV) had a smaller direct bandgap than CQDs (3.95 eV), indicating that the introduction of PH resulted in a narrowed bandgap of CQDs [54]. Fig. 3e and f shows the Mott-Schottky plots of CQDs and CQDs-PH, respectively, to estimate their flat-band potentials. The positive slopes demonstrated that CQDs and CQDs-PH are n-type semiconductors, attributed to the electron-rich oxygen and nitrogen functionalities. Consequently, the flat-band potential is approximately at the conduction band potential (ECB) in the n-type semiconductor [55,56]. The ECB values of CQDs and CQDs-PH are roughly estimated as −0.82 and −1.06 V vs SCE, respectively. Based on the equation of ERHE = ESCE + 0.0591 pH + 0.24 and ERHE = ENHE + 0.0591 pH, the ECB values of CQDs and CQDs-PH are determined as −0.58 and −0.82 V vs NHE, respectively. CQDs-PH has a higher conduction band position, implying that photogenerated high-energy electrons have a stronger reductive ability to coordinate surface redox reactions [57].

Fig. 4 a shows MB degradation in different systems. The MB degradation efficiencies of CQDs/PMS and CQDs-PH/PMS are 15.6% and 30.8% in dark (Fig. 4a, left), respectively, indicating that the introduction of PH induced new active sites and promoted direct electron transfer from CQDs-PH to PMS to generate ROS. In addition, the unsatisfactory catalytic activity of pristine CQDs may be associated with the overabundant oxygen groups (32.18 at.%), giving rise to the poor conductivity and occupation of surface active carbon sites. Compared with CQDs/PMS, the MB removal rate was significantly accelerated under LED illumination and complete degradation was attained within 10 min in the CQDs-PH/PMS system. The photocatalytic degradation process is illustrated in Video 1, where the MB solution quickly faded within 2 min. The excellent photooxidation performance is ascribed to the introduction of PH. The formation of C=N bonds as surface polarized sites greatly inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and promotes the separation of charge carriers for PMS decomposition and photodegradation.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101546

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Video 1

Video 1

The impact of CQDs-PH dosage on MB degradation in the CQDs-PH/PMS system is shown in Fig. S3. As the concentration of CQDs-PH increases from 0.1 to 2 g/L, the adsorption of MB did not increase apparently (less than 10%), but the removal efficiencies of MB increased significantly. The supply of a greater catalyst dosage will generate more photoinduced electrons and holes on photoexcited CQDs-PH, and the photogenerated electrons will be captured by PMS to produce more ROS. With a CQDs-PH dose of 0.2 g/L, MB was completely removed within 10 min. Considering the high efficiency of CQDs-PH and avoiding light shadowing in the presence of overabundant photocatalyst, an optimal dosage of 0.5 g/L was adopted to investigate other reaction factors, mechanisms, and the reusability of the CQDs-PH/PMS system.

The adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of MB was studied at its different initial concentrations ranging from 10 to 50 mg/L (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). The results showed that MB adsorptive removal (mg/L) gradually increased (from 10 to 42 mg/L) with the increase of initial MB concentration from 10 to 50 mg/L [58]. However, the photodegradation efficiency decreased from 100% to 83.7% with an increased MB concentration, due to the insufficient ROS which are quickly consumed by excessive MB and intermediates. On the one hand, high-concentration MB molecules may cover the active sites on CQDs and limit light penetration, thus prohibiting photo-charge generation and the interaction with PMS to generate ROS on CQDs-PH surface. On the other hand, the competition between overabundant MB molecules and decolorized intermediates with limited ROS may further decrease the decolorization efficiency at a higher MB concentration. The impacts of various background inorganic anions, including Cl−, SO42−, NO3− and HCO3−, were evaluated on photocatalytic decontamination. As shown in Fig. S6, apart from HCO3−, the other inorganic anions had little inhibition on PMS activity. When HCO3− was introduced into the solution, only 57.5% of MB was removed in 60 min. This may be explained by the fact that HCO3− greatly competed for O2 •− to form the less active carbonate radical (CO3•−) through Eq. (1) [59,60].
(1a) HCO3− + O2 •− → CO3•− + HO2 −

To evaluate the stability of CQDs-PH, cyclic tests of CQDs-PH/PMS were performed for five successive runs (Fig. 4b). The degradation efficiency of MB slightly reduced after each cycle, which may be attributed to mild surface corrosion and degradation of PH functionality on CQDs-PH by ROS, thus deteriorating the performance in photocarrier generation and transfer. However, after five cycles of experiment, the degradation efficiency of MB maintains above 92%, which proves that the quantum photocatalyst (CQDs-PH) has good reusability.

The impacts of initial pH on photocatalytic degradation of MB by PMS alone or CQDs-PH/PMS were also investigated. In order to compare the effect of initial pH more intuitively, the amount of catalyst was fixed at 0.1 g/L. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, at the pH value lower than pKa1 of PMS (pKa = 9.4), PMS mainly exists in the form of SO5 2− with a similar redox property [61]. However, with the increase of solution pH, the MB degradation efficiencies are 80.3% (pH = 5), 74.4% (pH = 6), 68.7% (pH = 7), 83.0% (pH = 8) and 92.8% (pH = 9) in CQDs-PH/PMS. The results showed that high degradation efficiencies could be achieved in both acidic and alkaline media. The degradation efficiency reached the highest at pH 9.0, indicating that alkaline conditions can promote ROS production in the CQDs-PH/PMS system. The enhanced performance can be attributed to the altered surface chemistry of CQDs-PH which may optimize photoactivity and facilitate the interaction with the positively charged MB.

The PL measurement was employed to investigate photoinduced charge carriers' separation or recombination efficiency at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm in Fig. 5b. The PL intensity decreases in both acidic and alkaline environments, attributed to the changed surface charges of acidic functionalities (e.g., C–OH and C–OOH) via protonation and deprotonation processes [62,63]. The solution pH will regulate the charge state of oxygen groups, and these polarized charged sites are beneficial to the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs on CQD-PH, thus improving hole oxidation and PMS activation through photoelectron transfer and photocatalytic reduction reactions.

Degradation of other organic compounds was also examined in the photooxidation system, and the pseudo-first-order rate constants of photodegradation were depicted in Figs. S7–8. The different degradation rate constants indicate that the CQDs-PH/PMS system exhibit substrate-specific reactivity [29]. Specifically, the CQDs-PH/PMS system exhibits higher degradation efficiency on organic dyes than phenolics. Compared with CQDs, the CQDs-PH/PMS system attained higher effectiveness in pollutant removal, indicating that PH functionalization promoted photocatalytic PMS activation to generate more ROS.

Radical trapping experiments were performed to identify ROS involved in MB decolorization and photocatalytic degradation. In this study, scavengers such as silver nitrate (SN), ammonium oxalate (AMO), L-histidine (His) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were employed to capture the photogenerated electron (e−), hole (h +), singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide anion radical (O2 •−), respectively. Methanol (MA) was used as the scavenger for sulfate (SO4 •−) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) because of the high reactivity toward both SO4 •− (K = 3.2 × 106 M−1 s−1) and •OH (K = 9.7 × 108 M−1 s−1). In contrast, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) is more selective to •OH (3.8–7.6 × 108 M−1 s−1) than to SO4 •− (K = 4.0–9.1 × 105 M−1 s−1) [64]. As depicted in Fig. 6 a and b, neither TBA nor MA was inhibitory on MB degradation in CQDs/PMS or CQDs-PH/PMS under LED light, indicating that SO4 •− and •OH are not the primary ROS. On the contrary, the presence of BQ significantly reduced MB removal rate in both systems, indicating that O2 •− had a significant contribution to MB degradation. Compared with CQDs, the presence of AMO had a greater inhibitory effect on MB degradation in the CQDs-PH/PMS system at the early stage, indicating that h + accounted for MB decomposition. However, the rapid consumption of h + synergistically reduced the recombination of electrons and holes, and the accumulated photoelectrons participated in PMS activation to generate more ROS, thereby accelerating MB degradation in the later stage. The intensified radical oxidation offset the inhibited hole oxidation with the presence of AMO. In addition, the addition of SN consumed photogenerated electrons and obviously inhibited MB degradation in the CQDs/PMS system. However, the presence of SN only slightly inhibited MB degradation in the first 10 min due to quenching photoelectrons in the CQDs-PH/PMS system, while the degradation accelerated subsequently. Thus, the quenching of photogenerated electrons reduced activation of PMS at the initial stage, but the facilitated charge separation and accumulation of h + remarkably boosted MB oxidation on CQDs surface. Additionally, h + can directly react with PMS to generate persulfate radicals (SO5 •−, 1.1 V), which may also contribute to MB removal.

The effect of various quenchers on PMS degradation was also investigated in the absence of MB. As shown in Fig. S7, His would directly consume PMS, so it may not be a suitable scavenger for singlet oxygen. Since the lifetime of 1O2 in D2O (20 μs) is 10 times as high as that in H2O (2 μs), singlet oxygenation will be theoretically intensified in D2O [36]. Therefore, MB degradation was investigated in the CQDs-PH/PMS/D2O system (Fig. 6c). When the solvent was exchanged from H2O to D2O, the MB degradation effect was not significantly improved, which indicates that 1O2 did not have a primary contribution to photodegradation. Moreover, we applied N2 purging to drive the dissolved oxygen out of the solution, and the degradation rate did not decrease significantly. Additionally, the photodegradation rate did not increase significantly with O2 bubbling. These results suggest that O2 •− evolved from the reaction between CQDs-PH and PMS, rather than dissolved oxygen. In-situ EPR spectra were collected using DMPO as spin trapping reagents for •OH, SO4 •− and O2 •− to further confirm the involved ROS. As shown in Fig. 6d, no signals for DMPO-•OH and DMPO-SO4 •− were observed when DMPO was added to the reaction after visible light irradiation, indicating that neither •OH nor SO4 •− was produced in the reaction. Fig. 6e shows the characteristic ESR signal of the DMPO-O2 •− adducts with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 generated in the presence of both CQDs-PH and PMS, while the signal is silent for PMS alone or CQDs-PH alone. This demonstrates the generation of O2 •− via PMS activation over the CQDs-PH catalyst. Therefore, the primary ROS are O2 •− and h + in the CQDs/PMS system under LED irradiation. Besides, the influences of various quenchers on PMS consumption were also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 6f, PMS would not be decomposed with CQDs-PH in the absence of a scavenger in the dark.

In contrast, obvious consumption of PMS was observed under LED irradiation, attributed to PMS activation by the photogenerated electrons to generate O2 •−. The addition of BQ consumes a large amount of superoxide radicals, thus further promoting the degradation of PMS. In addition, the presence of AMO enhances PMS decomposition, which implies that the introduction of AMO may promote the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs by directly consuming h +, thereby securing the sustainable supplement of photogenerated electrons for activating PMS to produce O2 •−. However, compared with CQDs-PH alone, the combination of SN and CQDs-PH reduces the consumption of PMS, which is attributed to the quenching of photogenerated electrons that hinder PMS decomposition. These quenching and PMS consumption outcomes are consistent with MB degradation profiles as aforementioned.

Steady photoluminescence (PL) analysis can reveal the recombination frequency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, indirectly reflecting the photocatalytic activity [65,66]. Fig. 7 a depicts the PL spectra of CQDs and CQDs-PH under 420 nm of light excitation. The PL results suggest that CQDs and CQDs-PH samples exhibit a broad peak at around 500 nm, ascribed to the radiative recombination between excited electrons and holes. The PL intensity significantly decreased for CQDs-PH, confirming that PH functionalization remarkably inhibits the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. The PL spectra for CQDs-PH in water with/without the addition of PMS are illustrated in Fig. 7b. The PL intensity of CQDs-PH in the aqueous solution with PMS is lower than that in the absence of PMS. With the extension of illumination, PL intensity further decreased in the CQDs-PH/PMS system. This indicates that the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes is suppressed in the presence of PMS. Thus, as an electron acceptor, PMS inhibits the recombination of excited electrons and holes, benefiting the effective utilization of both photoinduced e− for PMS activation and holes for direct oxidation. Subsequently, PMS will be activated by irradiated CQDs-PH to produce O2 •− via photocatalytic reduction to decompose MB in the solution.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7c, CQDs-PH exhibits intensified photocurrent responses compared with CQDs, indicating that a greater number of electron-hole pairs were generated in CQDs-PH, which were efficiently separated and migrated compared with CQDs. In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is exploited to reflect the samples' conductivity and interfacial charge transfer behavior (Fig. 7d). The EIS profile of CQDs-PH shows a smaller semicircle diameter than CQDs, indicating the higher conductivity and lower charge-transfer resistance of CQDs-PH, which warrants the efficient transportation and separation of charge carriers in CQDs-PH. In summary, the optical and electrochemical measurements show that the introduction of PH in CQDs is highly conductive to promoting the generation (photo-absorption), separation and migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs for surface redox reactions.

In order to further explain the different photocatalytic activities upon the change of surface state regulated by surface functionalization of CQDs, we carried out quantum chemical calculations using density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) method [44]. The ground state geometry optimization and excited state calculations are optimized at B3LYP/6-311 g (d, p) and CAM-B3LYP/6-311 g (d, p) levels, respectively. Multiwfn is used to perform electron-hole analysis of the first five excited states of CQDs and CQDs-PH [45]. CQDs are modeled using a graphene fragment, and the edges are functionalized with –COOH, –OH, –C=O and C–O–C to mimic different oxygen functionalities. Adsorption energy (Eads) of PMS, length of S–O (ls-o) and O–O (lo-o) onto CQDs and CQDs-PH were calculated. Compared with the adsorption energy (Eads) of CQDs/PMS in Fig. 8 a, the Eads values of CQDs-PH/PMS decreased from −0.811 to −1.001 eV, which indicated that CQDs-PH exhibited a better PMS adsorption capability. Moreover, as summarized in Table 1 , the lo-o bonding of PMS in CQDs/PMS (1.443 Å) and CQDs-PH/PMS (1.442 Å) is smaller than the free PMS molecule (1.456 Å, Fig. S10). Thus, PMS is would not decompose into SO4 •− and •OH− in the CQDs-PH/PMS system. In contrast, PMS in CQDs-PH/PMS has the longest ls-o (1.781 Å); the cleavage of S–O bond in PMS would generate HO2 • which further transfers to O2 •−.

Moreover, theoretical calculations at the TDDFT level on electron-hole distribution were carried out. As shown in Fig. 9 , the isosurface plot of the electron-hole distribution in each figure is shown in the left column. In showing the main characteristics of the hole-electron diagram more clearly and intuitively, the distributions of holes and electrons are equivalently described as an ellipse shape, where D index is defined as the distance between the centers of the two ellipses, reflecting the separation efficiency of electrons and holes. Compared with CQDs, the PH modification remarkably promotes the separation of electron-hole pairs. Especially, S0→S5 excited states showed phenomenal changes in the electron-hole distribution, resulting in a significant increase in the D index from 0.491 to 3.351 Å. The augmented charges separation indicated that the electron-hole distribution is significantly separated upon light irradiation. In addition, the energy of the excited state (Exc. Energy), coulomb attraction energy between electrons and holes (CAE), and the overall average distribution of electrons and holes (H indices) of the first five excited states of CQDs and CQDs-PH are listed in Table 2 for comparison.

Compared with CQDs, the excitation energy of the first five excited states of CQDs-PH become smaller, which implies that the introduction of PH intensifies the visible light response of CQDs, making them easier to absorb and leverage more photons in the solar light spectrum. In addition, the CAE value of CQD-PH decreases, indicating the weakened coulombic attractions between electrons and holes, which is conducive to the separation of electron-hole pairs upon excitation. The quantum chemical calculations results are consistent with the experimental and characterization findings.

To further investigate the role of the PH group, we defined two fragments of CQDs-PH. The first fragment is the graphene domain of CQDs, and the second fragment is PH functionality (Fig. 10 a). The contributions of the different molecular fragments to the hole, electron, hole-electron overlap, and electron-hole difference in the S0→S5 excited state are listed in Table 3 . The contributions of the molecular fragments are plotted on a heat map with the horizontal coordinates corresponding to the fragment numbers (Fig. 10b).

The electron-hole analysis shows that 95.45% of the electrons are accumulated on the graphitic domain of CQDs, and only 4.55% of the electrons are distributed in the PH group. Up to 29.44% of the holes are distributed on the PH group and 70.56% in the graphitic domain of CQDs. Thus, the integral value of the electron-hole density difference in the PH group is negative, indicating that the PH group loses electrons and some of the electrons transfer to the framework of CQDs. The single-electron excitation is examined, which shows that the PH group loses 0.2489 e− during the electron excitation process, which transfers to the carbon framework of CQDs, thus realizing the spatial separation of photoinduced charge carriers.

Based on the above discussion, a possible mechanism for ROS generation and photocatalytic degradation of pollutants in the CQDs-PH/PMS system is proposed in Fig. 11 . Under visible light irradiation, electrons transition from the valence band of CQDs-PH to the conduction band. Meanwhile, the photogenerated electrons were separated from h+ and transferred from the PH group to CQDs to react with the surface-adsorbed PMS to generate O2 •−. On the other hand, holes remaining in the PH group of CQDs-PH will directly react with MB for rapid decolorization, giving rise to the excellent photocatalytic performance of the CQDs-PH/PMS system. The modification of PH remarkably broadened visible light absorption and facilitated charged separation/migration at the surface region of CQDs-PH, boosting the activation of PMS and photooxidation of MB.

We prepared oxygen-functionalized CQDs using a facile thermal treatment method and selectively tuned the surface chemistry of CQDs by substituting the ketone groups with PH to prepare CQDs-PH. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared CQDs-PH was significantly enhanced compared to pristine CQDs. The reactive species in the CQDs-PH/PMS system were identified as superoxide radicals and photoinduced holes. Superoxide radicals are produced from PMS activation by photoelectrons rather than from dissolved oxygen. Experimental characterization and theoretical studies show that PH modified the surface state of CQDs to extend the absorption in the visible light region, facilitated the separation of electron-hole distribution at the excited state, and promoted charges migration to different directions to trigger hole oxidation and PMS reduction by electrons. The PH modification remarkably improves the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs for MB oxidation. This study will provide a facile strategy to regulate the surface chemistry and optical properties of carbon quantum materials with enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability for sustainable water purification.

Wenyuan Han: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - Original Draft; Degang Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing, Project administration; Ximin Hu: Formal analysis, Investigation; Wenwu Qin: Validation, Formal Analysis; Hongqi Sun: Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing; Shaobin Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - Review & Editing; Xiaoguang Duan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.
